hypothesis: Using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for real-time image analysis of histopathology slides leads to more accurate cancer diagnosis compared to traditional feature extraction methods when evaluated on a dataset of 10,000 labeled histopathology images.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for cancer diagnosis using histopathology images
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Feature Extraction for Histopathology Classification

### 1. Data Strategy

This study uses a dataset of 10,000 labeled histopathology images collected from multiple medical institutions over 5 years. Each image is labeled with cancer type and stage by board-certified pathologists. To ensure label quality, a stratified 10% subset (1,000 images) is independently reviewed by two pathologists; inter-rater agreement is computed, and any images with disagreement are flagged for exclusion to prevent label noise from inflating performance estimates.

Known data quality issues are addressed proactively: (1) stain variation across institutions is corrected using Macenko stain normalization, applied consistently pre-model; (2) out-of-focus and non-tissue regions are identified via automated focus detection and cropped; (3) images with inadequate quality metrics are excluded, and the number of exclusions per institution is documented to identify systematic problems.

### 2. Split Strategy

A stratified split is employed, grouping images by institution and cancer type/stage combination. Within each stratum, 60% of images are randomly assigned to the training set, 15% to the validation set, and 25% to the test set. This approach prevents institutional and class-imbalance confounds: all cancer types and all institutions appear in each set at representative frequencies, avoiding spurious model performance inflated by high-prevalence classes or institution-specific artifacts. No temporal split is used because images lack a temporal ordering that would predict future outcomes; stratification by institution and class is the appropriate control.

The split is finalized before any model training begins. Random seeds are fixed and documented to ensure reproducibility.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the CNN model, raw RGB pixel intensities from 1024×1024 tissue regions serve as input. The model automatically learns hierarchical convolutional features. Pre-processing (stain normalization, focus detection, per-channel intensity normalization) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent data leakage.

For the baseline (traditional feature extraction + logistic regression), hand-crafted features are computed on training images: color histograms (64 bins per channel), local binary patterns (LBP across multiple radii), Haralick texture statistics, and morphological features (nuclei density via watershed segmentation). These features are computed identically for all three sets but normalized (z-score) using training set statistics only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a ResNet-50 CNN pre-trained on ImageNet, with the final classification layer adapted for the dataset's cancer types and stages. The baseline is a logistic regression classifier trained with default hyperparameters, specifically using an L2 regularization strength of C=1.0. This provides a practical baseline without the confound of manual hyperparameter tuning, which can introduce variability into model comparison. Both models undergo equivalent preprocessing and are evaluated on the same splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of learned convolutional features versus hand-crafted features, with confounds held constant.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is accuracy on the test set. This metric is chosen because it provides a clear, interpretable percentage of correct classifications, which is simple to communicate to stakeholders and aligns well with common expectations in model performance reporting.

Secondary metrics include per-class sensitivity and specificity (to identify if any cancer type is systematically under- or over-detected), calibration metrics (to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true likelihoods, essential for clinician confidence), and institution-stratified confusion matrices (to verify generalization across imaging protocols).

### 6. Validation Approach

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Models are trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set; the model state yielding the best accuracy on validation is selected. The test set is sealed and evaluated only once after model finalization, preventing overfitting to test data and ensuring an unbiased estimate of deployment performance.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Institutional imaging variation is controlled via stratified splits and stain normalization. Patient demographics (age, comorbidities) are excluded from model inputs, preventing demographic shortcuts to diagnosis; however, a post-hoc subgroup analysis stratifies test performance by patient age and comorbidity groups to detect whether demographic bias emerges despite feature exclusion. Class imbalance is addressed via per-class metrics rather than accuracy, ensuring all cancer types contribute equally to the reported performance.

The experiment establishes whether CNN-based feature learning outperforms hand-crafted features when both are given equal preprocessing and tuning effort on this multi-institutional dataset. It does not establish whether the CNN generalizes to unseen institutions or imaging modalities outside the training data distribution—that would require external validation.

### 8. Final Test Evaluation

After both models are finalized, they are applied to the test set in a single run. Accuracy, per-class sensitivity/specificity, and calibration metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Confusion matrices are stratified by institution and cancer type. Results are compared via McNemar's test (for paired proportions) to determine whether the CNN significantly outperforms the baseline. All code, hyperparameters, and random seeds are documented for reproducibility.

planted_issues.description: The baseline model uses a logistic regression with fixed default hyperparameters (C=1.0), without any tuning. This creates an unfair comparison because the baseline is likely under-optimized, artificially inflating the relative performance of the more complex CNN model. A sound comparison requires both models to undergo similar tuning efforts to isolate the benefit of architecture rather than arbitrary parameter choices.

The primary evaluation metric is classification accuracy, which is inappropriate in a multi-class medical diagnosis context with potential class imbalance. Accuracy can mask poor performance on rare but clinically significant cancer subtypes by favoring majority classes. A balanced metric like macro-averaged F1 score is needed to ensure all cancer types are evaluated fairly, especially given the high stakes of false negatives.

must_not_claim.claim: The validation set should not be used for early stopping or model selection because it introduces bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The validation set is correctly used for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping in ML best practice. It is separate from the test set and properly sealed until final evaluation, so its use does not constitute data leakage or invalidation of results.

must_not_claim.claim: Fixing the baseline's regularization parameter without tuning makes the experiment more reproducible and reduces variability.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While reproducibility is important, fixing hyperparameters without tuning undermines fairness in model comparison. The sound design requires equivalent tuning for both models to isolate architectural contribution, and default settings are unlikely to be optimal across domains, leading to misleading conclusions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using accuracy is acceptable because it is simple to interpret and commonly used in clinical reporting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Although accuracy is interpretable, it is methodologically unsound for imbalanced medical datasets. The hypothesis emphasizes accurate diagnosis across *all* cancer types, and accuracy could hide poor performance on rare but critical subtypes. Domain-specific evaluation requires metrics robust to imbalance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies that the baseline model lacks hyperparameter tuning while the CNN does undergo tuning, making the comparison invalid. The critic argues that fair model comparison requires equivalent effort and optimization on both sides. The defense fails to justify this asymmetry, leading to rejection of the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that omitting tuning for one model biases the comparison in favor of the more complex model and contradicts the principle of controlling confounds.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies that accuracy is an unsuitable primary metric in a multi-class clinical setting with potential class imbalance, as it may hide poor performance on rare but important cancer subtypes. The critic argues that macro-F1 or another balanced metric must be used to ensure equitable evaluation across all classes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that accuracy can be inflated by performance on frequent classes while missing critical errors on rarer ones, contrary to the clinical goal of reducing false negatives for all cancers.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Critic identifies both flaws: the untuned baseline and the use of accuracy. The critic argues that together these flaws could doubly inflate the perceived performance gap between CNN and baseline, leading to erroneous conclusions about CNN superiority. Final verdict requires correction of both issues.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic establishes either flaw independently, and especially both jointly, undermines confidence in the experimental validity.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: CNN vs. Traditional Feature Extraction for Histopathology Classification

### 1. Data Strategy

This study uses a dataset of 10,000 labeled histopathology images collected from multiple medical institutions over 5 years. Each image is labeled with cancer type and stage by board-certified pathologists. To ensure label quality, a stratified 10% subset (1,000 images) is independently reviewed by two pathologists; inter-rater agreement is computed, and any images with disagreement are flagged for exclusion to prevent label noise from inflating performance estimates.

Known data quality issues are addressed proactively: (1) stain variation across institutions is corrected using Macenko stain normalization, applied consistently pre-model; (2) out-of-focus and non-tissue regions are identified via automated focus detection and cropped; (3) images with inadequate quality metrics are excluded, and the number of exclusions per institution is documented to identify systematic problems.

### 2. Split Strategy

A stratified split is employed, grouping images by institution and cancer type/stage combination. Within each stratum, 60% of images are randomly assigned to the training set, 15% to the validation set, and 25% to the test set. This approach prevents institutional and class-imbalance confounds: all cancer types and all institutions appear in each set at representative frequencies, avoiding spurious model performance inflated by high-prevalence classes or institution-specific artifacts. No temporal split is used because images lack a temporal ordering that would predict future outcomes; stratification by institution and class is the appropriate control.

The split is finalized before any model training begins. Random seeds are fixed and documented to ensure reproducibility.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For the CNN model, raw RGB pixel intensities from 1024×1024 tissue regions serve as input. The model automatically learns hierarchical convolutional features. Pre-processing (stain normalization, focus detection, per-channel intensity normalization) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied identically to validation and test sets to prevent data leakage.

For the baseline (traditional feature extraction + logistic regression), hand-crafted features are computed on training images: color histograms (64 bins per channel), local binary patterns (LBP across multiple radii), Haralick texture statistics, and morphological features (nuclei density via watershed segmentation). These features are computed identically for all three sets but normalized (z-score) using training set statistics only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The proposed model is a ResNet-50 CNN pre-trained on ImageNet, with the final classification layer adapted for the dataset's cancer types and stages. The baseline is a logistic regression classifier trained on hand-crafted features extracted via traditional image processing methods. Both models undergo equivalent hyperparameter tuning via grid search on the validation set: the CNN tuner optimizes learning rate, weight decay, batch size, dropout, and training epochs; the baseline tuner optimizes regularization strength (C) and feature subsets. Both receive identical preprocessing and are evaluated on the same splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the contribution of learned convolutional features versus hand-crafted features, with confounds held constant.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-averaged F1 score (F1_macro) on the test set. This metric is chosen because (1) it balances precision and recall, both critical in oncology—false negatives risk patient harm via misdiagnosis; false positives risk unnecessary treatment; (2) macro-averaging prevents high-frequency cancer types from dominating the metric, ensuring the model performs adequately on rare but clinically important subtypes; (3) it is threshold-independent and thus appropriate for clinical deployment where decision thresholds can be adjusted post-hoc.

Secondary metrics include per-class sensitivity and specificity (to identify if any cancer type is systematically under- or over-detected), calibration metrics (to ensure predicted probabilities reflect true likelihoods, essential for clinician confidence), and institution-stratified confusion matrices (to verify generalization across imaging protocols).

### 6. Validation Approach

The validation set is used exclusively for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Models are trained on the training set and evaluated on the validation set; the model state yielding the best F1_macro on validation is selected. The test set is sealed and evaluated only once after model finalization, preventing overfitting to test data and ensuring an unbiased estimate of deployment performance.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Institutional imaging variation is controlled via stratified splits and stain normalization. Patient demographics (age, comorbidities) are excluded from model inputs, preventing demographic shortcuts to diagnosis; however, a post-hoc subgroup analysis stratifies test performance by patient age and comorbidity groups to detect whether demographic bias emerges despite feature exclusion. Class imbalance is addressed via F1_macro and per-class metrics rather than accuracy, ensuring all cancer types contribute equally to the reported performance.

The experiment establishes whether CNN-based feature learning outperforms hand-crafted features when both are given equal preprocessing and tuning effort on this multi-institutional dataset. It does not establish whether the CNN generalizes to unseen institutions or imaging modalities outside the training data distribution—that would require external validation.

### 8. Final Test Evaluation

After both models are finalized, they are applied to the test set in a single run. F1_macro, per-class sensitivity/specificity, and calibration metrics are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals. Confusion matrices are stratified by institution and cancer type. Results are compared via McNemar's test (for paired proportions) to determine whether the CNN significantly outperforms the baseline. All code, hyperparameters, and random seeds are documented for reproducibility.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because both corruptions are methodologically subtle and could be justified superficially (e.g., 'simplicity' or 'reproducibility'), requiring domain knowledge in clinical ML to recognize their impact on fairness and metric validity.
